steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank ends one independent clause (“many are still well ______”) and must connect it to the next independent clause (“Fama Diagne Sène, for example, is a celebrated playwright from Senegal”). The second clause is an example that supports the first, so the punctuation must legally join two independent clauses while introducing that example.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'known:': a colon after an independent clause correctly introduces an example or elaboration. “many are still well known: Fama Diagne Sène, for example…” is grammatically complete and logical. ✓
  • Choice B — 'known and': “and” is a coordinating conjunction, but it creates faulty coordination because “many are still well known” and the second clause are not parallel in meaning — the second clause is an example, not a coordinate idea. ✗
  • Choice C — 'known,': a comma alone between two independent clauses is a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — 'known': no punctuation at all between two independent clauses creates a fused (run-on) sentence. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
